notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
her husband President Roosevelt's father
her husband President Roosevelt's Hartford-born mother

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States

notable.notable_blurb: 26th US President; Rough Rider; Nobel Peace laureate

cross_connections.display_label: his Harvard ornithology partner on an 1877 bird list
Trust-busted Morgan's Northern Securities Company in 1904
designed the townhouse his family used near the White House
drew up the guardianship papers for his children
the Columbia president and Nobel laureate he befriended

military_service.notes: Led the assault on the San Juan Heights, 1898; Medal of Honor awarded in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the childhood friend next door
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt married Edith Kermit Carow in London in 1886, his second wife and a granddaughter of the Union general Daniel Tyler. Through her he entered the Tyler family, with its Edwards and Hooker roots in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard ornithology friendship became his first publication
narrative_blocks.body: He met Henry Davis Minot, a Boston-based naturalist, as a Harvard freshman in 1877. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — his first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Trust-busted J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities Company
narrative_blocks.body: His administration brought the antitrust suit that dissolved Morgan's Northern Securities holding company in 1904, ending the merger of the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads. Morgan reportedly told him 'Send your man to my man and they can fix it up' — the President refused, and the Supreme Court sided with the government.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Columbia's president Nicholas Butler
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's circle included Nicholas Murray Butler, the long-serving Columbia president and 1931 Nobel peace laureate, whom he nicknamed Nicholas Miraculous. The two Republican allies stayed close from Roosevelt's rise through the White House years.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelts borrowed a famous architect's townhouse
narrative_blocks.body: When the White House was torn up for renovation in 1902, the family decamped to a townhouse belonging to the firm of Carrère and Hastings. Its partner Thomas Hastings would go on to design the New York Public Library and Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns.

notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

narrative_blocks.header: Only general in D-Day's first wave
narrative_blocks.body: At fifty-six, with a heart condition and walking with a cane, Roosevelt was the only general to land with the first wave at Utah Beach on June 6, 1944. He died of a heart attack five weeks later and received the Medal of Honor posthumously.

narrative_blocks.header: He turned a landing error into a foothold
narrative_blocks.body: When the first craft drifted a mile south of their mark, he walked the fire-swept beach, met each arriving regiment, and improvised the attack from where they actually stood. Bradley later called it the bravest thing he saw in the war.

narrative_blocks.header: A father-son Medal of Honor, a century apart
narrative_blocks.body: His posthumous Medal of Honor and his father's, awarded at last in 2001 for San Juan Hill, made the Roosevelts only the second father and son both to earn it. The other pair were the two MacArthurs.

narrative_blocks.header: Father and son both hit the beaches
narrative_blocks.body: He was the only father to land with a son on D-Day; his boy Quentin came ashore at Omaha while he led at Utah. The family's soldiering instinct had carried into a second world war.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead of a heart attack five weeks later
narrative_blocks.body: He never saw the medal coming; a heart attack killed him in Normandy about five weeks after the landing. He was buried near the front and later moved to lie beside his brother Quentin, lost flying in the first war.

bio.bio_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

military_service.awards: Distinguished Service Cross; French Croix de Guerre
military_service.notes: Wounded at Soissons and gassed at Cantigny.

military_service.awards: Medal of Honor (posthumous, presented to his widow September 1944)
military_service.notes: Operation Torch (North Africa), Tunisia, and Sicily before leading the first wave ashore at Utah Beach on D-Day; died of a heart attack at Meautis, France, July 12, 1944.

narrative_blocks.header: Red Cross and YMCA work across two wars
narrative_blocks.body: Eleanor ran YMCA canteens in France in the First World War and directed the American Red Cross Club in England in the Second. She organized the first American women's committee for China relief in 1937.

notable.notable_blurb: Photographer; first grandchild of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: Roosevelt's first grandchild, behind the camera
narrative_blocks.body: Grace was Theodore Roosevelt's first grandchild and trained at the Sorbonne as a freelance photographer. She gave the Library of Congress thousands of her own photographs alongside her mother's albums.

narrative_blocks.header: She gave 5,000 photographs to the nation
narrative_blocks.body: Over a long career she photographed presidents and visiting dignitaries up close. In 1986 she handed the Library of Congress some five thousand of her images, together with her mother's albums.

cross_connections.display_label: the fashion model who turned photographer

bio.bio_blurb: Photographer; first grandchild of Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Banker and Navy aviator; Roosevelt grandson

narrative_blocks.header: Banker who flew for the wartime Navy
narrative_blocks.body: After working at DuPont and serving as a Navy aviator in the Pacific, Roosevelt became a partner in the Philadelphia brokerage Montgomery, Scott. He also served as Pennsylvania's Secretary of Commerce.

bio.bio_blurb: Banker and Navy aviator; Roosevelt grandson

military_service.notes: Naval aviator and flag lieutenant in the Pacific theater; Air Medal.

notable.notable_blurb: CIA technical-services chief and art collector

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt who ran the CIA's gadget shop
narrative_blocks.body: Cornelius headed the CIA's Technical Services Division, the branch that built espionage devices and guarded against electronic eavesdropping. A mining engineer and Navy veteran, he gave the National Gallery a major collection of Escher prints.

narrative_blocks.header: The spymaster who collected Escher
narrative_blocks.body: The same mind that ran the CIA's technical shop built one of the great private hoards of M. C. Escher prints, alongside Japanese netsuke and Andean finds. He later gave more than fifty of the Escher works to the National Gallery of Art.

cross_connections.display_label: a Roosevelt cousin who ran the 1953 Iran coup
the Revolution's spymaster, Washington's Culper Ring chief

bio.bio_blurb: CIA technical-services chief; Roosevelt grandson

military_service.notes: Served in the Navy and Naval Reserve, 1942-1951.

notable.notable_blurb: Soldier and aviation executive; Roosevelt grandson

narrative_blocks.header: His plane went down off Hong Kong
narrative_blocks.body: Named for his uncle the fallen WWI aviator, Quentin landed at Omaha Beach on D-Day while his father landed at Utah. He became a China aviation executive and died at twenty-nine in a Hong Kong plane crash, an end echoing his namesake's.

narrative_blocks.header: At fourteen he found a new fossil species
narrative_blocks.body: As a boy Quentin explored an Arizona cave and unearthed an extinct pronghorn, described as a new species in a museum paper. At nineteen he traveled alone into western China and brought back a thousand rare Naxi manuscripts for the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: His Harvard thesis on a vanishing script
narrative_blocks.body: As a student he traveled to the Tibetan borderlands of western China and brought back rare Naxi manuscripts, whose pictographs faintly recall Egyptian hieroglyphs. He made them the subject of his Harvard senior thesis before the Army claimed him.

cross_connections.display_label: the WWI aviator uncle he was named for

bio.bio_blurb: Soldier and aviation executive; Roosevelt grandson

military_service.notes: Artillery officer; wounded at Kasserine Pass; landed in the first wave at Omaha Beach. Silver Star, Purple Heart, Croix de Guerre.

notable.notable_blurb: Explorer and author; son of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He died by his own hand in Alaska
narrative_blocks.body: Kermit joined his father on the African safari and the near-fatal Amazon expedition, and fought in both World Wars, first in the British army. Long burdened by alcoholism and depression, he died by suicide at an army post in Alaska in 1943.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found a transatlantic steamship line
narrative_blocks.body: An explorer and writer, Roosevelt co-founded the Roosevelt Steamship Company, which became part of the United States Lines. He published books on hunting and travel.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept his father alive on the Amazon
narrative_blocks.body: On the 1914 expedition down Brazil's River of Doubt, Kermit rationed his own quinine to his malarial father and was credited with saving the former President's life. He nearly died of the fever himself.

narrative_blocks.header: He won the Military Cross in Mesopotamia
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 he took an honorary captaincy in the British Army and fought under General Maude in the Mesopotamian campaign, where he earned the Military Cross. He crossed to the American artillery in France for the war's last offensive.

narrative_blocks.header: He tore through Harvard in record time
narrative_blocks.body: A voracious reader, he compressed the four-year Harvard course into about two and a half years, breaking off after his freshman year to join his father's African safari. He took his degree in 1912.

cross_connections.display_label: re-ran the River of Doubt eight decades later

bio.bio_blurb: Explorer, soldier, son of Theodore Roosevelt

military_service.unit: 14th Light Armored Motor Battery, Machine Gun Corps
military_service.notes: Served under General Maude in the Mesopotamian campaign, 1917-1918.
Transferred to the American Expeditionary Force in France, 1918; Meuse-Argonne Offensive.
Intelligence officer; helped raise a territorial militia of Alaska Natives.

narrative_blocks.header: She built a school for disabled children
narrative_blocks.body: In memory of her mother, Belle donated family land to create the Belle Willard School, the first Fairfax County school built for special-needs children. It opened in 1957 with wheelchair ramps and therapy rooms.

narrative_blocks.header: She crossed party lines for FDR
narrative_blocks.body: Though the Oyster Bay Roosevelts were staunch Republicans, Belle campaigned for Franklin Roosevelt's reelection in 1944. Her father had owned Washington's Willard Hotel and served as ambassador to Spain.

cross_connections.display_label: also drawn by John Singer Sargent
another Willard who founded a pioneering school

bio.bio_blurb: Philanthropist; wife of Kermit Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: CIA officer who ran the 1953 Iran coup

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the CIA's 1953 Iran coup
narrative_blocks.body: Operating under a cover name, Kim Roosevelt orchestrated Operation Ajax, the CIA-British plot that overthrew Iran's prime minister and restored the Shah in 1953. Decades later he broke Agency tradition by publishing a book on how it was done.

narrative_blocks.header: He refused to repeat the trick in Guatemala
narrative_blocks.body: Asked to engineer a second coup in Guatemala in 1954, Roosevelt declined, judging that Iran had worked only because so many Iranians shared its aims. He left the Agency a few years later for the oil and defense industries.

cross_connections.display_label: a Roosevelt cousin who ran CIA technical services
the general who ran Army intelligence in two wars
Washington's spymaster who ran the Culper Ring
a cousin and fellow CIA Arabist officer

bio.bio_blurb: CIA officer who ran the 1953 Iran coup

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt who studied with Nadia Boulanger
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph trained as a pianist and composer, studying with the legendary Nadia Boulanger in France. He spent decades teaching music at the Hartt School, Mannes, and Longy.

narrative_blocks.header: He captained a warship supporting D-Day
narrative_blocks.body: Before his musical career Joseph commanded the destroyer transport USS Greene in the wartime Navy. His ship supported the Normandy landings.

narrative_blocks.header: He lectured on music at three universities
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his own compositions, Joseph earned diplomas and degrees from several conservatories. He lectured on music at Columbia, Fairleigh Dickinson, and the New York College of Music.

cross_connections.display_label: the modernist poet, his first wife's father
his first wife Nancy's mother
a fellow descendant who led choirs and wrote on music
the Dial editor who first married Nancy's mother
the Irish senator who last married Nancy's mother
the model who became Nancy's father's companion
a descendant who composed Jingle Bells

bio.bio_blurb: Pianist-composer and Navy commander; Roosevelt grandson

narrative_blocks.header: She campaigned for FDR against her own family
narrative_blocks.body: When the Oyster Bay Roosevelts backed Dewey in 1944, Clochette broke ranks and campaigned for her cousin Franklin's fourth term. She married John Gorham Palfrey, later dean of Columbia College.

bio.bio_blurb: Granddaughter of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He drove ambulances for the British Eighth Army
narrative_blocks.body: Discharged from the Army for illness soon after enlisting at eighteen in 1943, he joined the American Field Service instead. He drove an ambulance with the British Eighth Army up through Italy until the fighting ended.

narrative_blocks.header: Oxford honors in history after the war
narrative_blocks.body: With the war over he read history at Oxford for three years and took his degree with high honors. He had prepared at Buckley, Groton, and Brooks before everything was interrupted.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at twenty-eight in his Sutton Place bed
narrative_blocks.body: He was found dead one January morning in 1953 at his home on Sutton Place, the exact cause never established. He had lately come through surgery in England, where he was a postgraduate student at the University of London.

bio.bio_blurb: Kermit's son; Oxford historian and WWII volunteer

military_service.notes: Enlisted in the U.S. Army in 1943, discharged for illness, then drove an ambulance with the British Eighth Army in Italy.

narrative_blocks.header: She nursed the wounded in wartime France
narrative_blocks.body: Early in the First World War she went to Paris with her husband and worked at the American Ambulance Hospital while he operated on men brought in from the front. She came home to decades of civic and hospital work on Long Island.

narrative_blocks.header: She made Sagamore Hill a national shrine
narrative_blocks.body: More than anyone she pushed to preserve her father's home, Sagamore Hill, as a public historic site, and guarded the Roosevelt papers given to Harvard. In Oyster Bay, where she fought for low-income housing, neighbors called her the Queen of Oyster Bay.

narrative_blocks.header: One of the museum's first women trustees
narrative_blocks.body: She became one of the first two women on the board of the American Museum of Natural History, the institution her grandfather had helped found. She also chaired the Nassau County Nursing Service and led the county's Red Cross.

narrative_blocks.header: Painted in her Red Cross uniform
narrative_blocks.body: For her formal family portrait she chose to sit in her Red Cross uniform rather than evening dress and jewels. She gave the organization some sixty years and chaired its Nassau County work.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelt child who stayed close to home
narrative_blocks.body: Of all the president's children she was the steady one, anchoring the family at Oyster Bay long after her brothers scattered to wars and far places. She married the surgeon who would tend the wounded beside her in France.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, a New York eye-surgery pioneer
was her mother-in-law, painted by John Singer Sargent

bio.bio_blurb: TR's only surviving daughter by Edith Carow

narrative_blocks.header: Division surgeon under fire in France
narrative_blocks.body: He served fourteen months as surgeon of the Second Infantry Division, wounded in the field and decorated by France with the Croix de Guerre and the Legion of Honor. The American army added its Distinguished Service Medal.

narrative_blocks.header: He rebuilt hospital care across Long Island
narrative_blocks.body: For some thirty years after the war he led surgery at the Glen Cove hospital and drove the founding of a new laboratory there, later named in his honor. Friends raised a quarter of a million dollars to build it.

narrative_blocks.header: A hunter in his father-in-law's mold
narrative_blocks.body: Like Theodore Roosevelt he loved big game, stalking Kodiak bear in Alaska and filming moose on long northern trips. He titled his own war memoir Wade in, Sanitary.

bio.bio_blurb: Surgeon; TR's son-in-law and WWI division surgeon

military_service.notes: Division surgeon in France; Croix de Guerre, Legion of Honor, Distinguished Service Medal.

narrative_blocks.header: The nephew Quentin left everything to
narrative_blocks.body: He was the small boy who pointed at passing planes and called them Uncle Quentin, and the aviator left him his death benefits. He died at eight, four years after the uncle he adored.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's grandson; died in childhood

narrative_blocks.header: She seconded Nixon's nomination in 1960
narrative_blocks.body: A granddaughter of Theodore Roosevelt, she addressed the Republican convention and served for forty years as her state's Republican committeewoman. Washington's governor later named her a trustee of the state university.

narrative_blocks.header: Founder of an island health center
narrative_blocks.body: On Vashon Island she founded a community health center and pressed for cleaner air and water across the Northwest. The Roosevelt conservation streak ran strong in her.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Seattle house held a president's china
narrative_blocks.body: The home a celebrated architect built for her family held White House china from her grandfather's table, some of it now in the Smithsonian. She kept the Roosevelt past close at hand.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's granddaughter; historian and conservationist

narrative_blocks.header: Half of Brattleboro's club-champion couple
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband became the only pair to hold both the men's and women's golf championships at their Vermont country club. She had come a long way from the Oyster Bay of her grandfather.

narrative_blocks.header: A president's granddaughter turned Vermonter
narrative_blocks.body: She raised her family in Brattleboro while her husband built a long career in law and the statehouse. Both of them took honorary degrees from the state university.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's granddaughter; married Vermont senator Gannett

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the uncle lost over France
narrative_blocks.body: Quentin Roosevelt, the president's youngest son, was shot down over France in 1918. Five years later this niece was born and given his name to keep — Judith Quentin Derby.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into a dynasty of scientists
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband and his father both made their names studying the brain and the eye, a rare scientific lineage. She died in 1973, in her forties, the mother of three.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, who built the Ames room illusion
was her mother-in-law, wife of the Ames-room scientist

bio.bio_blurb: TR's granddaughter; wife of neuroscientist Del Ames

notable.notable_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

narrative_blocks.header: The only man twice ruined by one wound
narrative_blocks.body: A grenade in New Guinea in 1943 shattered the same knee German fire had wrecked in France in 1918, leaving him the only American ever rated totally disabled for one wound in two wars. He came home with four Silver Stars.

narrative_blocks.header: The old lion is dead
narrative_blocks.body: When Theodore Roosevelt died in January 1919, Archibald wired the news to his scattered brothers and sister in five blunt words. He was still recovering from his French wounds at the time.

narrative_blocks.header: From Teapot Dome to a bond house
narrative_blocks.body: He resigned from Sinclair Oil during the Teapot Dome inquiry and, though never implicated, gave testimony damaging to his former employer. After the second war he founded the municipal-bond firm Roosevelt and Cross, which still trades today.

narrative_blocks.header: A hard turn to the far right
narrative_blocks.body: In his later years he joined the John Birch Society and founded the Veritas Foundation to root out what he called socialist influence at Harvard. In 1954 he published a pamphlet attacking the award of a Theodore Roosevelt medal to the diplomat Ralph Bunche.

narrative_blocks.header: The last of Edith's sons
narrative_blocks.body: He outlived every brother, the only son of Theodore and Edith to come through both World Wars alive. His stone at Oyster Bay reads, the old fighting man home from the wars.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

military_service.notes: Severely wounded in France and discharged with full disability; two Silver Star citations and the Croix de Guerre.
Commanded in New Guinea; wounded at the ridge named in his honor.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in the car her husband was driving
narrative_blocks.body: In 1971 she died in an automobile crash near the couple's pre-Revolutionary house on Turkey Lane in Cold Spring Harbor, with Archibald at the wheel. They had married at Boston's Emmanuel Church in 1917.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Archibald Roosevelt, TR's son

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt who became a master Arabist
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his career as an intelligence officer and scholar of the Middle East, fluent in Arabic and steeped in the region's politics. His memoir traced a life lived largely in the shadows of the Cold War.

narrative_blocks.header: His son would steward the family name
narrative_blocks.body: His own son, born in 1942, grew up to chair the Theodore Roosevelt Association and carry the family's public legacy forward. The line of the president's soldier sons did not end on the battlefield.

cross_connections.display_label: a cousin and fellow CIA Middle East hand

bio.bio_blurb: TR's grandson; CIA officer and Arabist

narrative_blocks.header: Nine dark novels, then silence
narrative_blocks.body: Between 1950 and 1962 she published nine novels of psychological menace, full of characters with hidden and dangerous second selves, then stopped writing for good. Patricia Highsmith, who almost never reviewed anyone, praised her first book.

narrative_blocks.header: An early voice of lesbian pulp fiction
narrative_blocks.body: Her frank treatment of gay and lesbian lives made her one of the daring postwar writers who opened American fiction to those themes. Long forgotten, her books were rediscovered and reissued in the early two-thousands.

narrative_blocks.header: A debutante who danced in South America
narrative_blocks.body: She came out in New York society in 1937, then made her living as a professional dancer in South America and Canada. She gave it up in 1945 to marry an artist and move to Paris.

narrative_blocks.header: She played down the Roosevelt name
narrative_blocks.body: She wrote under her first husband's surname and downplayed her famous lineage, wanting the work judged on its own merits. She married three times and spent her last years alone in the North Carolina woods with cats and chickens.

bio.bio_blurb: Roosevelt granddaughter; lesbian pulp-fiction novelist

narrative_blocks.header: The quiet sister among soldiers and writers
narrative_blocks.body: Born between her decorated father's two wars, she lived more privately than her siblings and married into the Jackson family. She outlived all but the youngest of them.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Nuremberg prosecutor
was her mother-in-law, who lived to ninety-five

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Archibald Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: Her brief marriage to a Soviet defector
narrative_blocks.body: A Barnard graduate, she was married for four years to Alexander Barmine, a former Red Army general who had defected and become an American broadcaster. Their daughter Margot carried the Roosevelt name forward.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Archibald Roosevelt; Barnard graduate

notable.notable_blurb: Roosevelt's aviator son, killed in action 1918

narrative_blocks.header: Shot down behind enemy lines at twenty
narrative_blocks.body: On July 14, 1918, his patrol met German fighters over Chamery and his plane went down behind enemy lines. German troops buried the president's son with full military honors, and the grave became a shrine that thousands of American soldiers came to see.

narrative_blocks.header: The White House's most incorrigible small boy
narrative_blocks.body: He shot spitballs at the official presidential portraits, flung snowballs from the roof at passersby, and once gouged a baseball diamond into the South Lawn. His father, who quietly counted him the favorite, mostly delighted in it.

narrative_blocks.header: He left Harvard for the cockpit
narrative_blocks.body: Admitted to Harvard like his father and brothers, he dropped out in 1917 at his father's urging to train as a pursuit pilot. He shipped to France and eventually flew with the Ninety-fifth Aero Squadron.

narrative_blocks.header: Reinterred beside the brother he idolized
narrative_blocks.body: In 1955 his remains were moved to the Normandy American Cemetery to lie beside Ted, who had died after leading men ashore on D-Day. A Long Island airfield, and much later a shopping center, carried his name.

narrative_blocks.header: His death benefits went to a small nephew
narrative_blocks.body: He left what he had to his sister Ethel's young son, a boy who had taken to pointing at passing planes and calling them Uncle Quentin. The child would die only four years after Quentin himself.

cross_connections.display_label: a private killed near Saint-Mihiel that same 1918
his namesake nephew, also lost in an air crash

bio.bio_blurb: TR's youngest son; WWI pilot killed in France

military_service.awards: French Croix de Guerre with Palm
military_service.notes: Pursuit pilot shot down and killed over Chamery, France, July 14, 1918.

notable.notable_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He owned the packet ship West Point
narrative_blocks.body: Carow inherited the Kermit and Carow shipping line from Robert Kermit, his father's partner, and owned the Liverpool packet West Point through the 1860s. The Civil War's collapse of American shipping ruined the trade and his fortunes declined.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter became a president's wife
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows lived beside the Roosevelts in New York, and his daughter Edith grew up alongside young Theodore. She married him in 1886 and became First Lady fifteen years later.

bio.bio_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: From Paris polish to genteel poverty
narrative_blocks.body: Educated in Paris during her father the general’s European years, Gertrude married the shipping heir Charles Carow in 1859 — and watched his drinking dissolve the family fortune. Widowed in 1883, she eventually moved to Italy, where living was cheaper.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter grew up with the Roosevelts
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows were intimates of the East 20th Street Roosevelts, and her daughter Edith was raised in and out of Theodore’s nursery — becoming First Lady in 1901, six years after Gertrude died at Turin.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Charles Carow
was the mother of her husband Charles Carow

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: New York merchant and Chamber of Commerce president

narrative_blocks.header: He led New York's Chamber of Commerce
narrative_blocks.body: Carow built his fortune in the Kermit and Carow shipping line and presided over New York's Chamber of Commerce from 1840 to 1842. He was an original incorporator of the Bank of Commerce, a forerunner of today's Morgan banking house.

cross_connections.display_label: married Isaac Carow’s son Charles, the shipowner

bio.bio_blurb: New York merchant and Chamber of Commerce president

cross_connections.display_label: married Eliza Mowatt’s son Charles, the shipowner

orbit_connections.name: Theodore Roosevelt (X00744) — married great-granddaughter of Daniel Tyler's parents
orbit_connections.relationship: Through his parents Sarah Edwards Tyler (H00936) and Capt. Daniel Tyler, Brig. Gen. Tyler is an ancestor of Edith Kermit Carow (1861-1948), who became Theodore Roosevelt's second wife and First Lady.

notable.notable_blurb: Union Civil War general and ironmaster

cross_connections.display_label: built an iron empire in Gilded Age Los Angeles
was his nephew and rose to Union general too

bio.bio_blurb: Union general, ironmaster, founder of Anniston

military_service.unit: 1st Connecticut Infantry; division, Army of Northeastern Virginia
military_service.notes: Commanded a division at First Bull Run; led a brigade at the Siege of Corinth; briefly commanded Camp Douglas.

narrative_blocks.header: Blamed for the Union rout at Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: Tyler commanded a division at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 and absorbed much of the blame for the Union defeat, mustered out within weeks. Recommissioned in 1862, he led troops at the siege of Corinth and briefly ran the Camp Douglas prison camp.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Alabama city of Anniston
narrative_blocks.body: After resigning from the army in 1864, Tyler built blast furnaces and ran railroads, finding far more success in rails than in pig iron. In the 1870s he established the Woodstock Iron Company and helped found Anniston, named for his daughter-in-law Annie.

narrative_blocks.header: West Point artillerist who studied in France
narrative_blocks.body: Graduating from West Point in 1819, Tyler became an authority on artillery, studying at the French school at Metz and translating French gunnery manuals into English. His honesty inspecting private arms contractors may have stalled his promotion beyond first lieutenant.

crossConnections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
